subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Mayan Astronomy
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Mayan Astronomy.

hook.text: The Maya tracked Venus with astonishing accuracy—their tables predicted its cycle to within two hours over 500 years.

buildup.text: They built observatories like El Caracol at Chichén Itzá, with windows aligned to Venus's extreme positions.

discovery.text: The Dresden Codex, a surviving Maya book, contains Venus tables used for both astronomy and warfare planning.

twist.text: Venus was linked to war. Maya rulers timed battles to Venus's appearance—celestial events dictated when to attack.

climax.text: Maya math used zero and base-20 counting. Their calendar system tracked cycles spanning millions of years.

punchline.text: Venus guided their wars. The sky shaped an empire's decisions.

quiz.question: How did the Maya use their astronomical observations of Venus?
options: They timed warfare and political events to Venus's appearances
They used Venus to navigate across the Atlantic Ocean
They predicted earthquakes based on Venus's position
